title: LangGraph Crash Course - Agent Workflows in Python
summary: A crash course on LangGraph covering agent workflows in Python. Topics include environment setup, basic workflows, memory management, conditional routing, RAG integration, human-in-the-loop patterns, and graph visualization for building multi-step agentic systems.
writeup: A hands-on crash course in LangGraph, the lower-level Langchain-ecosystem library for modeling agent workflows as directed graphs with explicit nodes, edges, and typed state. The tutorial builds from a minimal single-node graph up through the graph compilation pattern, explaining why the MessagesState class uses an append annotation rather than a replace semantic. The through-line is that LangGraph trades LangChain's high-level convenience for precise control over durable, branching, and conditional processes.
key_points: LangGraph sits below LangChain in the same ecosystem: LangChain wraps LLM calls at a high level, while LangGraph models entire processes as stateful graphs with explicit nodes and edges, suited to complex or durable workflows. LangSmith is the third tool in the trio, handling monitoring and MLops.

Every LangGraph workflow is a directed graph starting at a reserved START node and ending at a reserved END node, with arbitrary intermediate nodes (each a plain Python function that receives the current state and returns an update) and edges (including conditional branches and loops) wired between them.

The MessagesState class from langraph.graph uses an annotated list with the add_messages reducer, which means returning a list of messages from a node appends to the existing message history rather than overwriting it, an essential distinction for building agents with conversation memory.

Building a graph follows a three-step pattern: construct a StateGraph with a state schema, add nodes and edges to the builder, then call compile() to get the runnable graph object that is invoked via graph.invoke() with an initial message dict.

Calling graph.invoke() in a naive while-loop does not produce a stateful conversation: each invocation is independent and receives only the messages passed in that call, so prior turns are invisible to the model unless the caller explicitly accumulates and passes them forward.

The uv package manager (uv init, uv add) is shown as an alternative to pip for isolated environments, but the tutorial notes plain pip install works identically for installing langraph, langchain, and python-dotenv.
